segments.title: Why the Leanders sit awkwardly between cruiser roles
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke frames the Leanders as ships caught between the scout-cruiser and first-class protected-cruiser roles.
segments.body: The comparison is a question of role fit, not a rejection of the Leanders as attractive or capable ships. Dr. Clarke argues that the light cruiser had absorbed the functions once divided among first- and second-class protected cruisers and scout cruisers. Measuring one design against that broad combined category therefore obscures the narrower job it most closely resembles. The Leanders sit between the scouting and first-class protected-cruiser ends of the spectrum, which explains why they can look awkward when judged by either extreme.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the class as a second-class protected-cruiser concept that sits between scout and first-class roles.

segments.title: A vanished cruiser role still shapes the design
segments.summary: The Leander concept retains the logic of a second-class protected cruiser even though that category had disappeared by the 1930s.
segments.body: In its earlier setting, a second-class protected cruiser could be exceptionally well suited to its defined role. By the 1930s, however, that formal category no longer existed within the light-cruiser system Dr. Clarke is describing. The disappearance of a label did not erase the professional experience formed while the older role structure was still familiar. Designers and admirals could therefore carry second-class protected-cruiser assumptions into a programme officially framed in newer terms.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts the old second-class protected-cruiser role with its disappearance in the 1930s and the influence of earlier professional experience.

segments.title: The Leander as a baseline for a cruiser programme
segments.summary: A middle-of-the-road Leander design could give a renewed light-cruiser programme room to develop in heavier or lighter directions.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents the class's middle position as a sensible place to restart a light-cruiser programme. A middle-of-the-road ship provides a practical baseline before the navy commits to a more specialized direction. The design could theoretically be enlarged toward the first-class protected-cruiser end of the spectrum, represented here by the Town class. It could instead be reduced toward a scout cruiser, making the Leander concept useful as the centre of a design family even if it does not define either extreme.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the Leander a sensible starting point and contrasts a larger Town-class direction with a reduced scout-cruiser direction.
